concepts.label: Field axioms of ℝ (addition and multiplication)
Order axioms and the ordered field structure of ℝ
Axiom of Completeness — statement and why ℚ fails it
Upper bound, lower bound, bounded above/below — definitions
Supremum (least upper bound) and infimum (greatest lower bound) — formal definitions
Epsilon characterisation of supremum: s = sup A iff s is an upper bound and ∀ε>0 ∃a∈A with a > s−ε
Archimedean Property — statement and proof sketch
Density of ℚ in ℝ — statement and proof
Nested Interval Property — statement and proof
Absolute value — definition and triangle inequality |a+b| ≤ |a|+|b|
Definition of a sequence; what it means for (aₙ) to converge to L (ε-N definition)
Uniqueness of limits — proof
Convergent sequences are bounded — proof
Algebraic Limit Theorem for sequences (sum, product, quotient)
Order Limit Theorem — if aₙ ≤ bₙ then lim aₙ ≤ lim bₙ
Squeeze Theorem for sequences
Monotone Convergence Theorem — bounded monotone sequences converge
Subsequences — definition; subsequences of a convergent sequence converge to the same limit
Bolzano-Weierstrass Theorem — every bounded sequence has a convergent subsequence (statement + proof)
Cauchy sequence — formal definition
Cauchy Criterion — a sequence converges iff it is Cauchy (proof both directions)
Series — definition via partial sums; convergence of a series
Cauchy criterion for series convergence
Absolute convergence implies convergence (and the converse fails)
Geometric series — when it converges and its sum
Open set — formal definition (every point is interior)
Closed set — definition as complement of open; equivalently contains all limit points
Arbitrary union of open sets is open; finite intersection of open sets is open
Limit point (accumulation point) — formal definition
Closure of a set — definition and characterisation
Compact set — definition via open covers (every open cover has a finite subcover)
Heine-Borel Theorem — compact ↔ closed and bounded in ℝ (proof)
Sequential compactness — a set is compact iff every sequence has a convergent subsequence in the set
Perfect set — closed set with no isolated points; example: Cantor set
Connected set — definition (not the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets)
Connected subsets of ℝ are exactly the intervals — proof
Functional limit — ε-δ definition of lim_{x→c} f(x) = L
Sequential characterisation of functional limits — lim f(x) = L iff f(xₙ) → L for every xₙ → c
Algebraic Limit Theorem for functions
Continuity at a point — ε-δ definition; three equivalent formulations
Composition of continuous functions is continuous
Extreme Value Theorem — continuous function on compact set attains its max and min (proof)
Intermediate Value Theorem — statement and proof
Uniform continuity — definition and difference from pointwise continuity
Continuous function on a compact set is uniformly continuous — proof
Types of discontinuity — removable, jump, essential; examples
Derivative — formal definition as a limit; differentiability at a point
Differentiable implies continuous — proof; converse fails (|x| at 0)
Algebraic differentiation rules — sum, product, quotient (proofs from definition)
Chain rule — statement and proof
Interior Extremum Theorem — if f has local max/min at c interior, then f′(c) = 0
Rolle's Theorem — statement and proof
Mean Value Theorem — statement, proof, and key applications
MVT applications: f′ > 0 implies increasing; f′ = 0 implies constant
L'Hôpital's Rule — rigorous statement, conditions, proof sketch
Taylor's Theorem with Lagrange remainder — statement and proof
Pointwise convergence of a sequence of functions — definition
Uniform convergence — definition and comparison with pointwise
Cauchy criterion for uniform convergence of function sequences
Uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous — proof
Uniform convergence allows interchange of limit and integral
Power series — definition, radius of convergence
Radius of convergence — Cauchy-Hadamard formula; behaviour at endpoints
Power series converges uniformly inside its radius of convergence
Power series can be differentiated term-by-term inside its radius of convergence
Partition of [a,b]; upper and lower sums U(f,P) and L(f,P)
Riemann integral — definition via sup of lower sums and inf of upper sums
Riemann integrability criterion — f integrable iff ∀ε>0 ∃P with U(f,P)−L(f,P) < ε
Continuous functions on [a,b] are Riemann integrable — proof
Monotone functions on [a,b] are Riemann integrable
Properties of the integral: linearity, monotonicity, additivity over intervals
Fundamental Theorem of Analysis Part 1 — if F(x) = ∫_a^x f(t)dt then F′(x) = f(x) (proof)
Fundamental Theorem of Analysis Part 2 — ∫_a^b f = F(b)−F(a) where F′ = f (proof)
Vector space — all 8 axioms (4 for addition, 4 for scalar multiplication)
Examples of vector spaces: ℝⁿ, ℂⁿ, polynomials 𝒫(𝔽), F^∞, function spaces
Basic properties from axioms: unique zero, unique additive inverse, 0·v = 0, (−1)v = −v
Subspace — definition and the three conditions to verify
Subspace examples and non-examples in ℝ² and ℝ³
Sum of subspaces U + W — definition; why it is the smallest containing subspace
Direct sum U ⊕ W — definition and characterisation (unique decomposition)
Direct sum criterion: U + W is direct iff U ∩ W = {0}
Span — definition; span is the smallest subspace containing the list
Finite-dimensional vs infinite-dimensional — definitions
Linear independence — formal definition; equivalent to no redundant vectors
Linear Dependence Lemma — if linearly dependent, one vector is in span of the others (proof)
Length of linearly independent list ≤ length of spanning list — proof
Basis — definition; equivalent characterisations (spanning + independent; unique representation)
Every finite-dimensional vector space has a basis (proof)
Every linearly independent list extends to a basis; every spanning list reduces to a basis
Dimension — well-defined (all bases have the same length); proof
dim(U) ≤ dim(V) for subspace U of V; equality implies U = V
dim(U + W) = dim U + dim W − dim(U ∩ W)
Linear map — definition (additivity + homogeneity)
Examples of linear maps: zero map, identity, differentiation, integration, matrix multiplication
Basic properties: T(0) = 0; T(au + bv) = aT(u) + bT(v)
Null space (kernel) of T — definition; null space is a subspace (proof)
Range (image) of T — definition; range is a subspace (proof)
Injectivity — definition; T injective iff null T = {0} (proof)
Surjectivity — definition; T surjective iff range T = W
Fundamental Theorem of Linear Maps (rank-nullity): dim null T + dim range T = dim V (proof)
Consequences of rank-nullity: map to smaller space not injective; map to larger space not surjective
Matrix of a linear map with respect to bases — definition and construction
Invertible linear map — definition; invertible iff injective and surjective
Isomorphism — finite-dimensional spaces of equal dimension are isomorphic
